attachment.content:
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
# UW Kubernetes Infrastructure Overview

This repo (`kubernetes-manifests`) is the source of truth for all Kubernetes cluster resources at UW. It is watched by [kube-applier](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/kube-applier) which applies changes to managed namespaces.

## Repository Structure

Top-level directories follow the pattern `<env>[-<number>]-<provider>`:

```
dev-aws/       dev-gcp/       dev-merit/
exp-1-aws/     exp-1-gcp/     exp-1-merit/
prod-aws/      prod-gcp/      prod-merit/
```

Inside each cluster directory are namespace directories, commonly prefixed with team name (e.g. `energy-`, `telecom-`).

## Providers

Three cluster providers:

| Provider | Type | Location |
|----------|------|----------|
| **aws** | Cloud (AWS) | eu-west-1 |
| **gcp** | Cloud (GCP) | europe-west |
| **merit** | On-premises (bare-metal) | Datacenter LD7 |

## Key Systems

- **kube-applier** — operator that watches this repo and applies changes
- **Wiresteward** — inter-cluster connectivity mesh (runs on every cluster)
- **Semaphore** — WireGuard-based federation between clusters
- **Strongbox** — secret encryption (decrypts on `git checkout`)
- **Thanos** — Prometheus HA/long-term storage setup
- **MetalLB** — bare-metal load balancer (Merit only)
- **Trident** — NetApp CSI driver for storage (Merit only)

## Access Control

- `CODEOWNERS` file controls access
- Owned by `@utilitywarehouse/system` team
- PRs discussed in #infra Slack

content.content:
1	# Glossary
2	
3	- **Merit** — On-premises (bare-metal) Kubernetes cluster provider, running in datacenter LD7. Treated as a first-class provider alongside AWS and GCP. Not a commercial product.
4	
5	- **Wiresteward** — WireGuard abstraction layer that serves dual purpose: (1) user-facing VPN to access UW internal networks (uses Okta for auth), (2) inter-cluster connectivity mesh between AWS, GCP, and Merit. Docs: https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/documentation/tree/master/infra/wiresteward
6	
7	- **Semaphore** — WireGuard-based federation system for cross-cluster communication. Each cluster has semaphore tokens/policies for the others.
8	
9	- **kube-applier** — Operator running inside the cluster that watches this git repo and applies changes to managed namespaces.
10

file.content:
# Glossary

- **Merit** — On-premises (bare-metal) Kubernetes cluster provider, running in datacenter LD7. Treated as a first-class provider alongside AWS and GCP. Not a commercial product.

- **Wiresteward** — WireGuard abstraction layer that serves dual purpose: (1) user-facing VPN to access UW internal networks (uses Okta for auth), (2) inter-cluster connectivity mesh between AWS, GCP, and Merit. Docs: https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/documentation/tree/master/infra/wiresteward

- **Semaphore** — WireGuard-based federation system for cross-cluster communication. Each cluster has semaphore tokens/policies for the others.

- **kube-applier** — Operator running inside the cluster that watches this git repo and applies changes to managed namespaces.

- **Strongbox** — Encrypted secret management. Secrets are encrypted at rest in the repo and decrypted on `git checkout` using keys in `~/.strongbox_keyring`.

- **Thanos** — Prometheus HA and long-term storage. Components: thanos-rule, thanos-query, etc.

- **MetalLB** — Bare-metal load balancer for on-prem Kubernetes. Used only on Merit clusters.

- **Trident** — NetApp CSI driver for storage. Used only on Merit clusters with NetApp ONTAP SAN.

- **LD7** — Datacenter identifier where Merit infrastructure is hosted.

- **Calico** — CNI plugin used for pod networking on Merit clusters.

- **Cumulus** — Linux-based network switch OS. Used for Merit's physical networking.

- **Matchbox** — Service for provisioning Flatcar/Container Linux hosts on Merit.

- **cfssl** — PKI/TLS toolkit used for certificate management on Merit.

- **kyverno** — Policy engine. Blocks namespace deletion unless annotated with `uw.systems/delete-allowed: "true"`.

- **Okta** — SSO provider for most UW services (Slack, Service Now, GitHub, etc.). Login at https://login.uw.systems.

- **GlobalProtect** — The "standard" UW VPN client. Wiresteward is the preferred alternative.

- **Service Now** — Help-desk platform for requesting access to software, equipment, etc.

- **1Password** — Password manager. Each team has vaults (e.g. Strongbox keys).

- **Strongbox** — Secret encryption tool (UW-built). Uses git filters to encrypt/decrypt files. Keys stored in `~/.strongbox_keyring` and team 1Password vaults.

file.content:
# Cluster Details

## DNS Patterns

```
*.dev.aws.uw.systems
*.exp-1.aws.uw.systems
*.prod.aws.uw.systems

*.dev.gcp.uw.systems
*.exp-1.gcp.uw.systems
*.prod.gcp.uw.systems

*.dev.merit.uw.systems
*.exp-1.merit.uw.systems
*.prod.merit.uw.systems
```

## Merit (On-Premises)

- **Datacenter**: LD7
- **Networking**: Cumulus Linux switches, Calico CNI (pod subnet `10.6.0.0/16`)
- **Load balancing**: MetalLB with BGP peers `10.89.4.253`, `10.89.4.254` (ASN 64512)
- **Public IP pool**: `85.91.53.1-85.91.53.127`
- **Internal IP pool**: `10.89.1.2-10.89.1.254`
- **Storage**: NetApp ONTAP SAN via Trident CSI
  - `netapp-ld7-kube-ext4` (default)
  - `netapp-ld7-kube-xfs`
- **API server**: `lb.master.prod.merit.uw.systems` (plain LB, no `elb.` prefix)
- **Provisioning**: Flatcar/Container Linux via Matchbox
- **PKI**: cfssl
- **Etcd**: self-managed cluster (`etcd-0/1/2.prod.merit.uw.systems`)
- **Network reachability**: Merit endpoints (`*.merit.uw.systems`) may not be reachable from outside the internal network. May require UW VPN or office network.

## AWS

- **API server**: `elb.master.k8s.<env>.aws.uw.systems`
- **Pod subnet**: `10.2.0.0/16`

## GCP

- **Pod subnet**: `10.4.0.0/16`

## Wiresteward

Runs as a pair per cluster: `0.private-wiresteward.<env>.<provider>.uw.systems` and `1.private-wiresteward.<env>.<provider>.uw.systems`. Provides inter-cluster connectivity across all providers. Exposes metrics on ports `8081` and `9100`.

2	
3	## Initial Onboarding (in order)
4	
5	1. **Install Git hooks**: `make hooks-install`
6	2. **Install kubectl**: `make kubectl-install-bin`
7	3. **Set up user**: Visit https://kube-auth-config.uw.systems (one-time)
8	4. **Set up cluster context**: `export EMAIL=you@uw.co.uk && make kube-config`
9	5. **Install CA certs**: `make ca-certs-install`
10	   - Merit clusters may be unreachable from outside internal network
11	6. **Authorization**: Add `RoleBinding` for your namespace (see `dev-aws/onboarding/auth.yaml`)
12	7. **Verify**: `kubectl --context=<context> -n <namespace> get pods`
13	
14	## Creating a New Namespace
15	
16	1. Add namespace to `<cluster>/kube-system/namespaces.yaml`
17	2. Create directory: `<cluster>/<team>-<name>/`
18	3. Include:
19	   - `limitrange.yaml`
20	   - `auth.yaml` (RoleBinding)
21	4. Update `CODEOWNERS` if needed
22	5. Add namespace to Prometheus targets (`thanos-shared/resources/prometheus.yaml.tmpl`)
23	6. Set up default network policy (allows inter-pod traffic + Prometheus scraping)
24	
25	## Deployment Workflow
26	
27	Two primary methods:
28	- **kube-applier** — watches this repo, auto-applies changes
29	- **GitHub Actions** — uses ServiceAccount + bearer token to PATCH deployments
30	
31	## Secrets Management (Strongbox)
32	
33	- Keys in `~/.strongbox_keyring`
34	- Decrypts on `git checkout`
35	- After adding new keys, must delete and re-checkout encrypted files:
36	  ```bash
37	  rm -r <secrets_folder> && git checkout -- <secrets_folder>
38	  ```
39	
40	## Alerts
41	
42	- Location: `<cluster>/sys-mon/thanos-shared/resources/alerts/`
43	- Must be added to `kustomization.yaml` as ConfigMap
44	- Must be mounted in `thanos-rule` under `rules-projected`
45	- All filenames must be unique (writes to same projected directory)
46	- Validate with: `promtool check rules <file>`
47	- Grafana OnCall integration configured in terraform repo
48	
49	## Deleting Namespaces
50	
51	Must annotate first (Kyverno policy blocks unannotated deletes):
52	```sh
53	kubectl --context <context> annotate ns <name> uw.systems/delete-allowed="true"
54	```

file.content:
# Common Workflows

## Initial Onboarding (in order)

1. **Install Git hooks**: `make hooks-install`
2. **Install kubectl**: `make kubectl-install-bin`
3. **Set up user**: Visit https://kube-auth-config.uw.systems (one-time)
4. **Set up cluster context**: `export EMAIL=you@uw.co.uk && make kube-config`
5. **Install CA certs**: `make ca-certs-install`
   - Merit clusters may be unreachable from outside internal network
6. **Authorization**: Add `RoleBinding` for your namespace (see `dev-aws/onboarding/auth.yaml`)
7. **Verify**: `kubectl --context=<context> -n <namespace> get pods`

## Creating a New Namespace

1. Add namespace to `<cluster>/kube-system/namespaces.yaml`
2. Create directory: `<cluster>/<team>-<name>/`
3. Include:
   - `limitrange.yaml`
   - `auth.yaml` (RoleBinding)
4. Update `CODEOWNERS` if needed
5. Add namespace to Prometheus targets (`thanos-shared/resources/prometheus.yaml.tmpl`)
6. Set up default network policy (allows inter-pod traffic + Prometheus scraping)

## Deployment Workflow

Two primary methods:
- **kube-applier** — watches this repo, auto-applies changes
- **GitHub Actions** — uses ServiceAccount + bearer token to PATCH deployments

## Secrets Management (Strongbox)

- Keys in `~/.strongbox_keyring`
- Decrypts on `git checkout`
- After adding new keys, must delete and re-checkout encrypted files:
  ```bash
  rm -r <secrets_folder> && git checkout -- <secrets_folder>
  ```

## Alerts

- Location: `<cluster>/sys-mon/thanos-shared/resources/alerts/`
- Must be added to `kustomization.yaml` as ConfigMap
- Must be mounted in `thanos-rule` under `rules-projected`
- All filenames must be unique (writes to same projected directory)
- Validate with: `promtool check rules <file>`
- Grafana OnCall integration configured in terraform repo

## Deleting Namespaces

Must annotate first (Kyverno policy blocks unannotated deletes):
```sh
kubectl --context <context> annotate ns <name> uw.systems/delete-allowed="true"
```

# Mobile Monorepo

[![CircleCI](https://circleci.com/gh/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile.svg?style=shield&circle-token=f77e472062f60f7ad1de471939f7d73a00f01757)](https://circleci.com/gh/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile)

A repository containing all things mobile. Also see [mobile manifests](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile-manifests).

# Table of Contents

1. [Makefile](#makefile)
1. [Deploying](#deploying)
1. [Repository Layout](#repository-layout)
1. [Using this Repository](#using-this-repository)
    1. [Protocol Buffers](#protocol-buffers)
        1. [GRPC Gateways](#grpc-gateways)
        1. [REST Clients](#rest-clients)
1. [Contributing](#contributing)
1. [Documentation](#documentation)

## Makefile

A [Makefile](./Makefile) exists in the root directory of the monorepo to help perform various tasks. Such recipes include:

| command | based on changes* | description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `make test` | ✓ |  executes unit tests for only changed packages | 
| `make test-all` || executes all unit tests | 
| `make sh-fmt` || executes [`sh-fmt`](https://github.com/mvdan/sh) for all shell files |
| `make proto-fmt` || formats Protocol Buffer (`protos/*.proto`) files (requires `clang-format` to be installed) |
| `make go-fmt` || executes [`gofumpt -s` and `gofumports -local`](https://github.com/mvdan/gofumpt) for non-generated Go code |
| `make fmt` || executes all formatters (`*-fmt` Makefile commands) |
| `make lint` || performs linting (see [.golangci.yaml](./.golangci.yaml)) |
| `make install` || downloads Go dependencies using `go mod download` |
| `make buf-gen` || generates Go Protocol Buffer libraries and Swagger definitions |
| `make clients` || generates REST clients for all Protocol Buffers with `service` definitions |
| `make generate` || executes `go generate ./...` |
| `make clean` || cleans up build artifacts |
| `make docker-up`|| starts the [docker-compose](./docker/docker-compose.yaml) stack |
| `make docker-down`|| stops the [docker-compose](./docker/docker-compose.yaml) stack |
| `make install-git-hooks` || installs optional Git hooks |
| `make install-git-message` || installs commit message template |
| `make check` || runs `make fmt lint test-all`. Useful for checking if CI will pass |
| `make circleci-build-changed` | ✓ | uses the circleci tool to execute the `buildChanged` CircleCLI workflow |
| `make circleci-build-all` || uses the circleci tool to execute the `buildAll` CircleCLI workflow |
| `make build-all-binaries` || builds changed all binaries |
| `make build-changed-binaries` | ✓ | builds changed Go binaries |
| `make build-container` || builds a single Docker container (takes a `service=my-service` argument) |
| `make build-containers` || builds containers for binaries contained in `bin/` |
| `make push-containers` || pushes containers for binaries contained in `bin/` |
| `make deploy-containers` || deploys containers for binaries contained in `bin/` to dev |
| `make containers-all` || compiles all Go binaries and builds all Docker containers |
| `make containers` | ✓ | compiles changed Go binaries and builds changed Docker containers |

*"based on changes" means that the command operates only on files which have been changed between `HEAD` and `origin/master`.

## Deploying

To deploy to `<environment>-dev` you will need to create a manifest in the [mobile manifests](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile-manifests)
repository. Upon merging your telecom-mobile pull-request CircleCI will automatically create an image tag patch using Kustomize and commit it to the master branch of mobile-manifests. Your change will then be picked up by ArgoCD and deployed. Deploying to production remains a manual task. For more information on deploying services to production see the documentation in the [mobile-manifests](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile-manifests) repository.
When deploying a new service don't forget to add it to the [list of services](https://wiki.uw.systems/posts/service-tiers-cqth110e).


## Repository Layout

* [cmd](cmd) - useful tools related to the monorepo or CI.
* [docker](docker) - Docker containers and docker-compose stacks.
* [docs](docs) - service documentation. Also contains API specifications.
* [githooks](githooks) - contains optional git hooks. Install with `make install-git-hooks`.
* [pkg](pkg) - importable mobile Go packages.
* [protos](protos) - Protocol Buffer contracts.
* [scripts](scripts) - useful scripts, mostly used by CI.
* [services](services) - services organised by deployment unit.

As a general rule, this repository follows the [Golang standards project layout](https://github.com/golang-standards/project-layout).

## Using this Repository

### Protocol Buffers

All Protocol Buffer contracts are stored in the `protos/` directory. We use a common naming convention to identify Protocol Buffers that belong to a service; for example the `OrderService` can be found under `services/order-service` and its corresponding Protocol Buffers can be found under `protos/order-service.proto`. Using such a naming convention allows for easy identification of ownership.

When authoring a new Protocol Buffer ensure that you correctly set the package name and options. This is especially important
when authoring a service definition. Below is an example from the `OrderService`:

```protobuf
syntax = "proto3";
package order_service;

option go_package = "github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile/pkg/order_service";

//...
```

**Note:** the name of the package (both `package` and `go_package`) use underscores, not hyphens.

#### GRPC Gateways

If your Protocol Buffer declares a `service` it's likely that you'll want a GRPC gateway deployed - running `make protos` automatically 
generates the corresponding `.pb.gw.go` file for you in the `pkg` directory for the service, as well as Swagger 
definitions (v2) under `docs/swagger/`. Swagger files are automatically copied into Docker containers under `/app/swagger.json` in line with our standard swagger-ui implementation.

**Note:** In order for the Swagger definitions to be correctly copied into Docker containers you must ensure that the package name in the Protocol Buffer file follows our naming convention.

#### REST Clients

In addition to GRPC Gateways, this repositories tooling can also automatically generate a corresponding REST Go client implementation using the OpenAPI spec. Executing `make clients` automatically builds all Protocol Buffers and generate clients for each service under the `pkg/clients/generated` directory.

## Contributing

Create a GitHub pull-request, please use the following template for your commit messages;

```text
# [<tag>] (If applied, this commit will...) <subject> (Max 72 char)
# |<----   Preferably using up to 50 chars   --->|<------------------->|
# Example:
# [feat] Implement automated commit messages

# (Optional) Explain why this change is being made
# |<----   Try To Limit Each Line to a Maximum Of 72 Characters   ---->|

# (Optional) Provide links or keys to any relevant tickets, articles or other resources
# Example: Github issue #23

# --- COMMIT END ---
# Tag can be
#    feat     (new feature)
#    fix      (bug fix)
#    refactor (refactoring code)
#    style    (formatting, missing semi colons, etc; no code change)
#    doc      (changes to documentation)
#    test     (adding or refactoring tests; no production code change)
#    version  (version bump/new release; no production code change)
#    dbg      (Changes in debugging code/frameworks; no production code change)
#    hack     (Temporary fix to make things move forward; please avoid it)
#    WIP      (Work In Progress; for intermediate commits to keep patches reasonably sized)
#    defaults (changes default options)
#
# Note: Multiple tags can be combined, e.g. [fix][test] Fix issue X and added test X
# --------------------
# Remember to:
#   * Capitalize the subject line
#   * Use the imperative mood in the subject line
#   * Do not end the subject line with a period
#   * Separate subject from body with a blank line
#   * Use the body to explain what and why vs. how
#   * Can use multiple lines with "-" or "*" for bullet points in body
# --------------------
```

You can do this automatically by using the `make install-git-message` utility which will present you with the correct
template when authoring a commit message

**DO NOT PUSH DIRECTLY TO MASTER.** Doing so will result in image tag patches becoming out-of-sync.

## Documentation
- [Technical diagrams](https://wiki.uw.systems/topics/technical-diagrams-t76b54dy)
- [List of services and tiers](https://wiki.uw.systems/posts/service-tiers-cqth110e)

content.content:
# js-mono ✨ **Powered by [Nx\*](https://nx.dev)** (\*batteries included) ✨ ## Prerequisites To get started with the monorepo project, ensure the following are installed and configured on your system: - **Node.js: Version 20.18.0 or higher.** [Download Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) - If you use `nvm`, you can simply run: ```bash nvm install & nvm use ``` - **Yarn**: Install Yarn globally using npm first: ```bash npm install -g corepack corepack enable yarn install ``` or ```bash npm install --global yarn corepack enable ``` - **NPM Registry auth** Add following line in your terminal's configuration (i.e. `~/.zshrc`) ```bash export NPM_TOKEN=token ``` You can get token from 1Pass vault, like shown here in [the example](https://wiki.uw.systems/posts/npm-tokens-16f6l31h#hf7k5-global-terminal-environment-config-file) - **Strongbox**: The keys in the env files are encrypted using Strongbox. In order to be able to decrypt them, you need to copy the JS Mono strongbox keys from 1password to your local ‘~/.strongbox_keyring’ file. After you have the keys added to your keyring run decrypt-secrets command. If everything went well, you should see key=value format in the env files instead of a single, large encrypted key. ```bash make decrypt-secrets ``` ## Install Dependencies To install the necessary dependencies, run: ```bash yarn install ``` _NB: If you run into problems with dependencies, you may try also:_ ```bash yarn clean yarn install ``` ## Running tasks with Nx To [execute tasks with Nx](https://nx.dev/features/run-tasks#running-tasks) use the following: ### Run a Single Task Syntax: ``` nx <...options> ``` Example: ``` // run test for my-app project nx test my-app // run eslint with --quiet attribute for my-app project nx lint my-app --quiet ``` ### Run Tasks for Multiple Projects Syntax: ``` nx run-many -t ``` Example: ``` // run typecheck for all projects in monorepo nx run-many -t typecheck // run lint, typecheck, test for all projects in monorepo nx run-many -t lint typecheck test ``` ### Run Tasks for Specific Projects Syntax: ``` nx run-many -t -p ``` Example: ``` // run typecheck for my-app and my-server projects nx run-many -t typecheck -p my-app my-server // run lint, typecheck, test for my-app and my-server projects nx run-many -t lint typecheck test -p my-app my-server ``` ### Run Tasks on Projects Affected by a PR Syntax: ``` nx affected -t ``` Example: ``` // run test for all affected projects by a PR nx affected -t test // run lint, typecheck, test for all affected projects by a PR nx affected -t lint typecheck test ``` [Learn more about the affected command »](https://nx.dev/ci/features/affected) Targets can be defined in the `package.json` or `projects.json`. Learn more [in the docs](https://nx.dev/features/run-tasks). ## Create a TypeScript Library in Nx monorepo #### This section explains how to add a TypeScript library using our custom Nx generator. ### Create private library Syntax: ``` nx g @js-mono/js:library ``` Replace `` with the name you want for your library. ### Create publishable library Configures the library ready for use with `yarn nx release` Syntax: ``` nx g @js-mono/js:library --publishable ``` Replace `` with the name you want for your library. ### What It Does - **Sets Up the Structure:** Creates a new folder under `libs/` with all the starter files. - **Configures Your Project:** Automatically sets up project settings and adds your new library to the monorepo’s project graph. - **Creates a Path Alias:** Adds an import alias (e.g.` @utilitywarehouse/my-lib`) so you can easily use the library in other apps and libraries across the monorepo. - **Keeps Things Consistent:** Sets up ESLint, Jest, and TypeScript to match our coding standards across the monorepo. ### Example: To create a library called `my-lib`, simply run: ``` nx g @js-mono/js:library my-lib ``` That’s it! After the command runs, you’ll have a new `libs/my-lib` folder with everything set up and ready for you to start coding! You can also easily import your newly created library into other apps or libraries within the monorepo using the alias, like this: ``` import { myLib } from "@utilitywarehouse/my-lib"; ``` ## Create a Next.js App in Nx monorepo #### This section explains how to add a Next.js application using our custom Nx generator. ### Create a Next.js app **Syntax:** ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next ``` Replace `` with the name you want for your application.\ This will scaffold a new Next.js app under `apps/` with the default configuration. ### Create a Next.js app with App Router Next.js introduced the App Router (`app/` directory) as the new routing system.\ You can enable it at generation time: ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next --appRouter ``` ### Add Playwright E2E Tests To bootstrap Playwright end-to-end tests alongside your new app: ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next --e2e ``` ### What It Does - **Sets Up the App Structure:** Creates a new folder under `apps/` with starter Next.js files. - **Configures Your Project:** Automatically configures Nx project settings for Next.js. - **Supports Both Routers:** By default uses the **Pages Router** (`pages/`), but you can switch to the App Router (`app/`) with `--appRouter`. - **Optional E2E Setup:** With `--e2e`, it adds a ready-to-use Playwright configuration and test suite. - **Keeps Things Consistent:** Sets up ESLint, Jest, and TypeScript to match our coding standards across the monorepo. ### Examples **Basic App:** ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next customer-portal ``` **With App Router:** ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next customer-portal --appRouter ``` **With Playwright E2E:** ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next customer-portal --e2e ``` **With App Router and Playwright E2E:** ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next customer-portal --appRouter --e2e ``` ## Create a GraphQL Server App in Nx monorepo #### This section explains how to add a GraphQL Server application using our custom Nx generator. ### Create a GraphQL Server app **Syntax:** ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:gqlserver ``` Replace `` with the name you want for your application.\ This will scaffold a new GraphQL server app under `apps/` with the default configuration and template. ### Examples ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:gqlserver my-gql-server ``` ### What It Does - **Sets Up the App Structure:** Creates a new folder under `apps/` with starter files. - **Configures Your Project:** Automatically configures Nx project settings for GQL Yoga server application. - **Keeps Things Consistent:** Sets up ESLint, Jest, and TypeScript to match our coding standards across the monorepo. - **Adds an Example Resolver:** Includes a sample resolver wired up with GraphQL Code Generator using the Server Preset from [GraphQL Yoga’s tutorial](https://the-guild.dev/graphql/yoga-server/tutorial/basic/11-setting-up-graphql-code-generator). This demonstrates how to integrate type-safe resolvers out of the box, ensuring you can start building queries and mutations with generated TypeScript types immediately. For more information, refer to the `README.md` file included in the generated project. ## Move Your Package/Library to JS-mono You can quickly migrate your existing package or library into JS-mono using our automated script. This process takes about a minute and gets you ready to work right away. ### How to Use Run the following command and follow the prompts: ```bash make move-to-js-mono ``` The script will ask you a few questions about your repository and the paths to your code. ### What Does the Script Do? - **Copies your code** from the specified repository into the `apps | libs` directory of JS-mono. - **Optionally preserves git history** if you choose. - **Copies only the relevant code** (e.g., source files and `package.json`), not the entire environment, CI, or framework files—JS-mono manages those for you. - **Ensures dependency management** by copying your library’s `package.json`. ### Example Migrations | Original Structure | Data Input | Result in JS-mono | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | | 
├── .github
├── .vscode
├── node_modules
├── src
│ └── feature.js
└── package.json
|
  • directories to transfer: `src`
  • `package.json` path: `package.json`
| 
├── [...All JS-mono code]
├── libs
│ └── example
│ ├── package.json
│ └── src
│ └── feature.js
└── package.json (JS-mono root)
| | 
├── .github
├── feature.js
└── package.json
|
  • directories to transfer: `./`
  • `package.json` path: `./package.json`
| 
├── [...All JS-mono code]
├── libs
│ └── flat
│ ├── package.json (lib one, should stay)
│ └── src
│ ├── .github (remove after)
│ ├── feature.js
│ └── package.json (lib one, should be removed)

# js-mono ✨ **Powered by [Nx\*](https://nx.dev)** (\*batteries included) ✨ ## Prerequisites To get started with the monorepo project, ensure the following are installed and configured on your system: - **Node.js: Version 20.18.0 or higher.** [Download Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) - If you use `nvm`, you can simply run: ```bash nvm install & nvm use ``` - **Yarn**: Install Yarn globally using npm first: ```bash npm install -g corepack corepack enable yarn install ``` or ```bash npm install --global yarn corepack enable ``` - **NPM Registry auth** Add following line in your terminal's configuration (i.e. `~/.zshrc`) ```bash export NPM_TOKEN=token ``` You can get token from 1Pass vault, like shown here in [the example](https://wiki.uw.systems/posts/npm-tokens-16f6l31h#hf7k5-global-terminal-environment-config-file) - **Strongbox**: The keys in the env files are encrypted using Strongbox. In order to be able to decrypt them, you need to copy the JS Mono strongbox keys from 1password to your local ‘~/.strongbox_keyring’ file. After you have the keys added to your keyring run decrypt-secrets command. If everything went well, you should see key=value format in the env files instead of a single, large encrypted key. ```bash make decrypt-secrets ``` ## Install Dependencies To install the necessary dependencies, run: ```bash yarn install ``` _NB: If you run into problems with dependencies, you may try also:_ ```bash yarn clean yarn install ``` ## Running tasks with Nx To [execute tasks with Nx](https://nx.dev/features/run-tasks#running-tasks) use the following: ### Run a Single Task Syntax: ``` nx <...options> ``` Example: ``` // run test for my-app project nx test my-app // run eslint with --quiet attribute for my-app project nx lint my-app --quiet ``` ### Run Tasks for Multiple Projects Syntax: ``` nx run-many -t ``` Example: ``` // run typecheck for all projects in monorepo nx run-many -t typecheck // run lint, typecheck, test for all projects in monorepo nx run-many -t lint typecheck test ``` ### Run Tasks for Specific Projects Syntax: ``` nx run-many -t -p ``` Example: ``` // run typecheck for my-app and my-server projects nx run-many -t typecheck -p my-app my-server // run lint, typecheck, test for my-app and my-server projects nx run-many -t lint typecheck test -p my-app my-server ``` ### Run Tasks on Projects Affected by a PR Syntax: ``` nx affected -t ``` Example: ``` // run test for all affected projects by a PR nx affected -t test // run lint, typecheck, test for all affected projects by a PR nx affected -t lint typecheck test ``` [Learn more about the affected command »](https://nx.dev/ci/features/affected) Targets can be defined in the `package.json` or `projects.json`. Learn more [in the docs](https://nx.dev/features/run-tasks). ## Create a TypeScript Library in Nx monorepo #### This section explains how to add a TypeScript library using our custom Nx generator. ### Create private library Syntax: ``` nx g @js-mono/js:library ``` Replace `` with the name you want for your library. ### Create publishable library Configures the library ready for use with `yarn nx release` Syntax: ``` nx g @js-mono/js:library --publishable ``` Replace `` with the name you want for your library. ### What It Does - **Sets Up the Structure:** Creates a new folder under `libs/` with all the starter files. - **Configures Your Project:** Automatically sets up project settings and adds your new library to the monorepo’s project graph. - **Creates a Path Alias:** Adds an import alias (e.g.` @utilitywarehouse/my-lib`) so you can easily use the library in other apps and libraries across the monorepo. - **Keeps Things Consistent:** Sets up ESLint, Jest, and TypeScript to match our coding standards across the monorepo. ### Example: To create a library called `my-lib`, simply run: ``` nx g @js-mono/js:library my-lib ``` That’s it! After the command runs, you’ll have a new `libs/my-lib` folder with everything set up and ready for you to start coding! You can also easily import your newly created library into other apps or libraries within the monorepo using the alias, like this: ``` import { myLib } from "@utilitywarehouse/my-lib"; ``` ## Create a Next.js App in Nx monorepo #### This section explains how to add a Next.js application using our custom Nx generator. ### Create a Next.js app **Syntax:** ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next ``` Replace `` with the name you want for your application.\ This will scaffold a new Next.js app under `apps/` with the default configuration. ### Create a Next.js app with App Router Next.js introduced the App Router (`app/` directory) as the new routing system.\ You can enable it at generation time: ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next --appRouter ``` ### Add Playwright E2E Tests To bootstrap Playwright end-to-end tests alongside your new app: ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next --e2e ``` ### What It Does - **Sets Up the App Structure:** Creates a new folder under `apps/` with starter Next.js files. - **Configures Your Project:** Automatically configures Nx project settings for Next.js. - **Supports Both Routers:** By default uses the **Pages Router** (`pages/`), but you can switch to the App Router (`app/`) with `--appRouter`. - **Optional E2E Setup:** With `--e2e`, it adds a ready-to-use Playwright configuration and test suite. - **Keeps Things Consistent:** Sets up ESLint, Jest, and TypeScript to match our coding standards across the monorepo. ### Examples **Basic App:** ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next customer-portal ``` **With App Router:** ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next customer-portal --appRouter ``` **With Playwright E2E:** ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next customer-portal --e2e ``` **With App Router and Playwright E2E:** ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:next customer-portal --appRouter --e2e ``` ## Create a GraphQL Server App in Nx monorepo #### This section explains how to add a GraphQL Server application using our custom Nx generator. ### Create a GraphQL Server app **Syntax:** ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:gqlserver ``` Replace `` with the name you want for your application.\ This will scaffold a new GraphQL server app under `apps/` with the default configuration and template. ### Examples ```bash nx g @js-mono/js:gqlserver my-gql-server ``` ### What It Does - **Sets Up the App Structure:** Creates a new folder under `apps/` with starter files. - **Configures Your Project:** Automatically configures Nx project settings for GQL Yoga server application. - **Keeps Things Consistent:** Sets up ESLint, Jest, and TypeScript to match our coding standards across the monorepo. - **Adds an Example Resolver:** Includes a sample resolver wired up with GraphQL Code Generator using the Server Preset from [GraphQL Yoga’s tutorial](https://the-guild.dev/graphql/yoga-server/tutorial/basic/11-setting-up-graphql-code-generator). This demonstrates how to integrate type-safe resolvers out of the box, ensuring you can start building queries and mutations with generated TypeScript types immediately. For more information, refer to the `README.md` file included in the generated project. ## Move Your Package/Library to JS-mono You can quickly migrate your existing package or library into JS-mono using our automated script. This process takes about a minute and gets you ready to work right away. ### How to Use Run the following command and follow the prompts: ```bash make move-to-js-mono ``` The script will ask you a few questions about your repository and the paths to your code. ### What Does the Script Do? - **Copies your code** from the specified repository into the `apps | libs` directory of JS-mono. - **Optionally preserves git history** if you choose. - **Copies only the relevant code** (e.g., source files and `package.json`), not the entire environment, CI, or framework files—JS-mono manages those for you. - **Ensures dependency management** by copying your library’s `package.json`. ### Example Migrations | Original Structure | Data Input | Result in JS-mono | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | |

content.content:
# Telecom Mobile Manifests

A repository containing all things fixed-line infra. This repository depends heavily on [Kustomize](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/kustomize), and this README assumes you have a working knowledge of Kustomize.

# Table of Contents

1. [Repository Layout](#repository-layout)
1. [Fixed Line Annotations](#fixed-line-annotations)

## Repository Layout

* [bases](bases) - contains the base (or "template") manifests for each deployable unit. Bases are faily generic and unopinionated in their configuration, whilst still having sane defaults. Bases can be deployed either as they are or with patches using overlays.
* [dev-merit](dev-merit) - contains kustomization manifests for dev-merit. Also contains patches to be merged into bases.

## Annotations

This repository uses custom annotations to modify manifests at compile-time.

| annotation | value | description |
| --- | --- | --- |

content.content:
Software development practices at UW
====================================

Introduction
------------

At UW in IT, we value the ability for teams to have maximum flexibility, with room to experiment, innovate,
learn and improve both in terms of processes and in terms of technology choices.

In practice, this means that we:

* Avoid standardisation for the sake of having standards.
* Encourage de-coupling from any particular vendor or product, in order to keep the cost of experimentation low.
* Avoid monocultures of approach or technology.

In a few limited areas, where we do see it necessary to standardise, we try to chose approaches or
technologies that impose very little constraint on teams. (Examples here are the adoption of docker and kubernetes).

Also, we have seen that over time, certain patterns, approaches, and technologies emerge as de-facto
standards, and in some of these cases, we benefit from the universality of approach. This document lists some of
those standard practices and some of the reasons behind adopting them.

This is not a rule-book, but a list of approaches that are becoming more widely used, and so in also using these
(unless you have a good reason not to) you can make your work more familiar and more approachable to others,
which especially helps when the need arises to interact or integrate across more than one team.

The basic high-level stuff
--------------------------

We try to run everything we can from docker images in our k8s cluster.

We prefer the flexibility and portability that comes with deploying as much as we sensibly can inside of k8s,
to the often simpler approach of relying on cloud vendor specific managed services. This means accepting a small
up-front and on-going overhead, in preference to a longer term, harder to measure exit cost in leaving a provider.
Remaining portable has greater value now that we have dev and prod environments in GCP as well as AWS.

We believe in micro-service architectures. It's important to understand the difference between a true microservices
architecture and a distributed monolith.

Solution & data architecture
----------------------------

We prefer event driven, ideally event sourced architectural approach. This keeps data modelling flexible, is
well suited to properly designed micro-service architectures, and is particularly well suited to migration efforts
such as the "getting out of Bill" work we are currently in the middle of.

We believe in domain driven design principles, and believe high level systems architecture should reflect the
structure of the business. We believe in individual system components having a well defined and understood
responsibility.

We do avoid sharing state between components through shared database or filesystem access.
Instead we prefer event passing.

Event modelling
---------------

We prefer fine grained events to coarse grained events. In other words, we represent a single change in a
single event where possible, and we are comfortable with having multiple events needed by a consumer to build
a complete or meaningful data set.  We also recognise that sometimes, multiple things can change in the same event.

We strongly prefer informative, not imperative events. For example, `OrderSubmitted` is an informative description of something that happened, whereas `CreateOrder` is an imperative to take an action. This is problematic because it implies that the event producer has to have an understanding of the possible outcomes. By writing consumers that are reactive to informative events, we are able to build flexible systems that can absorb complexity and changing requirements.

We design events to represent a business-meaningful change that happened in the real world. Sometimes, we supplement
these events with further (calculated) events that model important business concepts for us, where such calculation
is non-trivial and is a publicly (outside UW staff) concept (an example is "partner status").

Technical formats for events
----------------------------

### Protocol buffers

We prefer google protocol buffers for modelling our events, because they are readable and changeable by non-developers
and represent a compact, high performance format, with convenient code generation for developers.

We follow the google style guide for protocol buffers.

We typically put our events in envelopes and use `Any` to carry the payload. This gives good flexibility
(compared to `OneOf`) at the cost of a slightly larger payload.  Teams often have their own envelopes for
internal use, but for cross team or cross-domain usage, we try to stick to a single shared envelope format found
at https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/event-envelope-proto

We model enums in protobuf where the `0` value represents `none` or `default` or `unknown` etc, rather than a real value.

We prefix enums in protobuf with the type name due to how protobuf namespaces work. (If this creates stuttering names
after generation, look at alternative generator options)

Many teams prefer keeping their proto code away from generated code, for a few reasons. One reason is that different
applications using the protos may have reasons to use different proto generators, or different versions or
configurations of proto generators, and this can result in incompatible code, often that compiles but fails in
interesting ways at runtime. Another reason is that we don't all use Go. We also use protobuf from js and starting
to from Rust. We don't want all of this in a single repo.

We find that generating code in the application that uses it avoids various problems associated with shared code.

### JSON

Where we can't use protocol buffers (for example 3rd party integration) we try to use JSON if possible.

We also use JSON internally in some cases. (TODO: where and why?)

In practice, this means that we:

* Avoid standardisation for the sake of having standards.
* Encourage de-coupling from any particular vendor or product, in order to keep the cost of experimentation low.
* Avoid monocultures of approach or technology.

In a few limited areas, where we do see it necessary to standardise, we try to chose approaches or
technologies that impose very little constraint on teams. (Examples here are the adoption of docker and kubernetes).

Also, we have seen that over time, certain patterns, approaches, and technologies emerge as de-facto
standards, and in some of these cases, we benefit from the universality of approach. This document lists some of
those standard practices and some of the reasons behind adopting them.

This is not a rule-book, but a list of approaches that are becoming more widely used, and so in also using these
(unless you have a good reason not to) you can make your work more familiar and more approachable to others,
which especially helps when the need arises to interact or integrate across more than one team.

The basic high-level stuff
--------------------------

We try to run everything we can from docker images in our k8s cluster.

We prefer the flexibility and portability that comes with deploying as much as we sensibly can inside of k8s,
to the often simpler approach of relying on cloud vendor specific managed services. This means accepting a small
up-front and on-going overhead, in preference to a longer term, harder to measure exit cost in leaving a provider.
Remaining portable has greater value now that we have dev and prod environments in GCP as well as AWS.

We believe in micro-service architectures. It's important to understand the difference between a true microservices
architecture and a distributed monolith.

Solution & data architecture
----------------------------

We prefer event driven, ideally event sourced architectural approach. This keeps data modelling flexible, is
well suited to properly designed micro-service architectures, and is particularly well suited to migration efforts
such as the "getting out of Bill" work we are currently in the middle of.

We believe in domain driven design principles, and believe high level systems architecture should reflect the
structure of the business. We believe in individual system components having a well defined and understood
responsibility.

We do avoid sharing state between components through shared database or filesystem access.
Instead we prefer event passing.

Event modelling
---------------

We prefer fine grained events to coarse grained events. In other words, we represent a single change in a
single event where possible, and we are comfortable with having multiple events needed by a consumer to build
a complete or meaningful data set.  We also recognise that sometimes, multiple things can change in the same event.

We strongly prefer informative, not imperative events. For example, `OrderSubmitted` is an informative description of something that happened, whereas `CreateOrder` is an imperative to take an action. This is problematic because it implies that the event producer has to have an understanding of the possible outcomes. By writing consumers that are reactive to informative events, we are able to build flexible systems that can absorb complexity and changing requirements.

We design events to represent a business-meaningful change that happened in the real world. Sometimes, we supplement
these events with further (calculated) events that model important business concepts for us, where such calculation
is non-trivial and is a publicly (outside UW staff) concept (an example is "partner status").

Technical formats for events
----------------------------

### Protocol buffers

We prefer google protocol buffers for modelling our events, because they are readable and changeable by non-developers
and represent a compact, high performance format, with convenient code generation for developers.

We follow the google style guide for protocol buffers.

We typically put our events in envelopes and use `Any` to carry the payload. This gives good flexibility
(compared to `OneOf`) at the cost of a slightly larger payload.  Teams often have their own envelopes for
internal use, but for cross team or cross-domain usage, we try to stick to a single shared envelope format found
at https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/event-envelope-proto

We model enums in protobuf where the `0` value represents `none` or `default` or `unknown` etc, rather than a real value.

We prefix enums in protobuf with the type name due to how protobuf namespaces work. (If this creates stuttering names
after generation, look at alternative generator options)

Many teams prefer keeping their proto code away from generated code, for a few reasons. One reason is that different
applications using the protos may have reasons to use different proto generators, or different versions or
configurations of proto generators, and this can result in incompatible code, often that compiles but fails in
interesting ways at runtime. Another reason is that we don't all use Go. We also use protobuf from js and starting
to from Rust. We don't want all of this in a single repo.

We find that generating code in the application that uses it avoids various problems associated with shared code.

### JSON

Where we can't use protocol buffers (for example 3rd party integration) we try to use JSON if possible.

We also use JSON internally in some cases. (TODO: where and why?)


Integration with Bill
---------------------

TODO


Use of data from Bill
---------------------

Event "fabrication"
-------------------

TODO

Projections, sharing & duplication
----------------------------------

TODO

Asynchronous communication
--------------------------

version: "3.1"

services:
  zookeeper:
    container_name: zookeeper
    image: wurstmeister/zookeeper
    ports:
      - "2181:2181"

  kafka:
    container_name: kafka
    image: wurstmeister/kafka
    ports:
      - "9092:9092"
    environment:
      KAFKA_ZOOKEEPER_CONNECT: zookeeper:2181
      KAFKA_LISTENERS: "INTERNAL://kafka:9090,EXTERNAL://0.0.0.0:9092"
      KAFKA_ADVERTISED_LISTENERS: "INTERNAL://kafka:9090,EXTERNAL://localhost:9092"
      KAFKA_LISTENER_SECURITY_PROTOCOL_MAP: "INTERNAL:PLAINTEXT,EXTERNAL:PLAINTEXT"
      KAFKA_INTER_BROKER_LISTENER_NAME: INTERNAL
      AUTO_CREATE_TOPICS: "true"

  kafka-ui:
    container_name: kafka-ui
    image: obsidiandynamics/kafdrop
    ports:
    - 9000:9000
    environment:
      KAFKA_BROKERCONNECT: "kafka:9090"
      JVM_OPTS: "-Xms32M -Xmx64M"
      SERVER_SERVLET_CONTEXTPATH: "/"

  mongo:
    hostname: mongo
    container_name: mongo
    image: mongo:4.2.0
    ports:
      - 27017:27017
    entrypoint: [ "/usr/bin/mongod", "--bind_ip_all"]

input.content:
# telecom-mobile

Go monorepo for all mobile services at UW.
Module: `github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile`
CI: CircleCI. Only changed packages are tested/built per run.

## Layout

```
cmd/        CLI tools and CI utilities
services/   ~66 deployable services (one dir per service)
pkg/        shared Go packages (imported by services)
internal/   shared code not exported outside the module
protos/     Protocol Buffer definitions (.proto files)
docker/     Dockerfiles and docker-compose stack
scripts/    CI/build scripts
docs/       API specs, Swagger, architecture docs
buf/        buf tool config for proto generation
```

Each service under `services/` typically contains:
```
cmd/        main entry point
internal/   service-private packages
pkg/        service-exported packages (if any)
README.md
```

## Key Services (sample)

- `sim-activation-service` — SIM activation flow
- `porting-service` — number porting
- `public-graphql` — external-facing GraphQL API
- `internal-graphql` — internal GraphQL API
- `account-gateway` — account-level routing
- `tariff-catalogue` — tariff data
- `esim-stock-service` — eSIM inventory
- `roaming-billing` — roaming charge processing
- `wholesale-state-api` / `wholesale-state-projector` — wholesale state
- `my-services-api` / `my-services-api-projector` — customer-facing services API
- `bill-connector` — integration with billing system
- `fraud-detection-ee-sms` — SMS fraud detection

## Common Make Targets

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `make build-all-binaries` | Build all Go binaries (use to verify env) |
| `make build-changed-binaries` | Build only changed binaries (vs origin/master) |
| `make test` | Test only changed packages |
| `make test-all` | Test all packages |
| `make lint` | Run golangci-lint |
| `make fmt` | Run all formatters (gofumpt, sh-fmt, proto-fmt) |
| `make check` | fmt + lint + test-all (CI equivalent) |
| `make buf-gen` | Generate Go code and Swagger from .proto files |
| `make clients` | Generate REST clients from OpenAPI specs |
| `make generate` | Run `go generate ./...` |
| `make docker-up` | Start local dev stack (Kafka, Mongo, etc.) |
| `make docker-down` | Stop local dev stack |
| `make install-git-hooks` | Install optional git hooks |
| `make install-git-message` | Install commit message template |

## Local Dev Stack (docker-compose)

`make docker-up` starts:
- **Kafka** on `localhost:9092` (Zookeeper on 2181)
- **Kafka UI** (Kafdrop) on `localhost:9000`
- **MongoDB** on `localhost:27017`

## Commit Message Format

```
[<tag>] <Subject starting with verb> (max 72 chars)

Optional body explaining why (not what).
Optional links to tickets.
```

Tags: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `style`, `doc`, `test`, `version`, `dbg`, `hack`, `WIP`, `defaults`
Multiple tags allowed: `[fix][test]`
Use `make install-git-message` to get the template in git.

## Deploy Flow

1. Merge PR to master → CircleCI builds changed service images
2. CircleCI commits image tag patch to `telecom-mobile-manifests` (dev-merit overlay) via Kustomize
3. ArgoCD picks up the commit → auto-deploys to dev
4. Production deploy is **manual** — update `prod-aws` overlay in `telecom-mobile-manifests`

**DO NOT push directly to master** — image tags in manifests will go out of sync.

## Protocol Buffers

- `.proto` files live in `protos/`
- Naming convention: service `foo-bar` → `protos/foo-bar.proto`, package `foo_bar`, go_package `github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile/pkg/foo_bar`
- `make buf-gen` generates Go code into `pkg/` and Swagger into `docs/swagger/`
- `make clients` generates REST clients into `pkg/clients/generated/`
- Swagger JSON is copied into Docker images at `/app/swagger.json`

# telecom-mobile-manifests

Kustomize-based Kubernetes manifests for the Mobile team's services.
Watched by ArgoCD (not kube-applier — unlike `kubernetes-manifests`).

## Layout

```
bases/        Base manifests for each deployable unit (generic, sane defaults)
dev-merit/    Overlays for dev-merit cluster
prod-aws/     Overlays for prod-aws cluster
templates/    Templates for postgres, mongodb, mongodb-ui
```

Each service has its own directory under `bases/` and each environment overlay.
A typical overlay is minimal — mostly image tag patches generated by CI.

## Deploy Flow

### Dev (automatic)
On merge to master in `telecom-mobile`:
1. CircleCI builds the Docker image
2. CircleCI commits an image tag patch to this repo's `dev-merit/<service>/` overlay
3. ArgoCD detects the commit and syncs the deployment to `dev-merit`

### Production (manual)
1. Update the image tag in `prod-aws/<service>/` (or let a deploy script do it)
2. Merge to master
3. ArgoCD syncs to `prod-aws`

## Environments

| Directory | Cluster | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `dev-merit/` | dev-merit (on-prem) | Auto-deployed by CI |
| `prod-aws/` | prod-aws (AWS eu-west-1) | Manual promotion |

## Secrets

Secrets in `dev-merit/secrets/` and `prod-aws/secrets/` are Strongbox-encrypted.
Decrypt with keys from the team's 1Password vault → `~/.strongbox_keyring`.

## Adding a New Service

1. Create `bases/<service-name>/` with deployment, service, kustomization.yaml
2. Create `dev-merit/<service-name>/` overlay (image tag, env patches)
3. Create `prod-aws/<service-name>/` overlay
4. Ensure the service is registered in the ArgoCD app config (`dev-merit/argocd-app.yaml` / `prod-aws/argocd-app.yaml`)
5. Register the service in the [service tiers list](https://wiki.uw.systems/posts/service-tiers-cqth110e)

# js-mono

Nx-powered TypeScript/JavaScript monorepo for UW frontend and Node services.
Node 20, Yarn (corepack), Nx for task orchestration.

## Layout

```
apps/       Deployable applications
libs/       Shared libraries (importable across apps and libs)
contracts/  Cross-team API contracts
tools/      Custom Nx generators and workspace tooling
scripts/    Setup and migration scripts
```

### Apps

| App | Description |
|---|---|
| `my-account-web` | Main customer-facing web app (Next.js) |
| `my-account-web-e2e` | Playwright end-to-end tests for my-account-web |
| `smart-tips-graphql` | GraphQL server for smart tips feature |
| `game-of-thrones-api` | (Example/test app) |

### Libs

| Lib | Description |
|---|---|
| `apollo` | Shared Apollo GraphQL client config |
| `tracing` | OpenTelemetry tracing setup |
| `fastify-uw-session-plugin` | Fastify plugin for UW session handling |
| `eslint-plugin` | Custom ESLint rules |
| `prettier-config` | Shared Prettier config |

## Setup

```bash
# Install deps
yarn install

# Decrypt Strongbox secrets (keys from JS Mono vault in 1Password → ~/.strongbox_keyring)
make decrypt-secrets

# Set npm registry token (in ~/.zshrc)
export NPM_TOKEN=<token from 1Password>
```

## Running Tasks with Nx

```bash
# Single task
yarn nx <target> <project>
yarn nx dev my-account-web
yarn nx test my-account-web

# Multiple projects
yarn nx run-many -t lint typecheck test

# Only affected by current branch
yarn nx affected -t test
```

### my-account-web Cheat Sheet

| Task | Command |
|---|---|
| Dev server | `yarn nx dev my-account-web` |
| Lint | `yarn nx lint my-account-web` |
| Type check | `yarn nx typecheck my-account-web` |
| Unit tests | `yarn nx test my-account-web` |
| E2E (Playwright) | `yarn nx e2e my-account-web-e2e` |
| GraphQL codegen | `yarn nx gql my-account-web` |
| Docker build | `yarn nx docker:build my-account-web` |
| Docker run | `yarn nx docker:run my-account-web` |

## Generating New Code

```bash
# New TypeScript library
yarn nx g @js-mono/js:library <name>
yarn nx g @js-mono/js:library <name> --publishable   # for npm release

# New Next.js app
yarn nx g @js-mono/js:next <name>
yarn nx g @js-mono/js:next <name> --appRouter        # App Router
yarn nx g @js-mono/js:next <name> --e2e              # with Playwright

# New GraphQL server
yarn nx g @js-mono/js:gqlserver <name>
```

## Migrating an Existing Package In

```bash
make move-to-js-mono
```
Interactive script — copies code into `libs/`, optionally preserves git history.

## Secrets

Env files are Strongbox-encrypted. After adding keys to `~/.strongbox_keyring`:
```bash
make decrypt-secrets
```

# Architecture Conventions

Engineering principles at UW. Not rigid rules but the de-facto standards the Mobile team follows.

## Core Principles

- **Microservices** — each service has a single, well-defined responsibility
- **Event-driven / event-sourced** — prefer async event passing over synchronous state sharing
- **Domain-driven design** — system boundaries reflect business structure
- **No shared state** — services must not share databases or filesystems; communicate via events

## Events

### Style
- **Informative, not imperative**: `OrderSubmitted` not `CreateOrder`
- **Fine-grained**: one event = one business-meaningful change
- **Business-meaningful**: model real-world changes, not internal implementation details

### Format
- **Protobuf** preferred for all internal events (compact, typed, code-gen friendly)
- Events are wrapped in envelopes; use `Any` for payload (more flexible than `OneOf`)
- Shared cross-team envelope format: `github.com/utilitywarehouse/event-envelope-proto`
- **JSON** used only for 3rd-party integrations or where proto is impractical
- Enum `0` value = `none` / `unknown` / `default` (never a real value)
- Enum values are prefixed with the type name (e.g. `STATUS_ACTIVE` not `ACTIVE`)

### Proto conventions (telecom-mobile)
- File: `protos/<service-name>.proto`
- Package: `<service_name>` (underscores)
- go_package: `github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile/pkg/<service_name>`
- Generate with `make buf-gen`; don't commit generated code separately

## Transport

- **Kafka** — primary event bus for async inter-service communication
- **gRPC** — synchronous service-to-service calls (generated from protobuf service definitions)
- **GraphQL** — external and internal API surfaces (`public-graphql`, `internal-graphql`)
- **REST** — used for 3rd-party integrations; REST clients auto-generated via `make clients`

## Projectors / Sinks Pattern

Common pattern in the codebase:
- `<service>-projector` — consumes events and builds a read model / projection
- `<service>-sink` — consumes events and writes to an external system (e.g. billing, CRM)
- `<service>-connector` — bridges two systems (e.g. `bill-connector`, `mso-connector`)

## Deployment

- All services run as Docker containers in Kubernetes
- Prefer k8s over cloud-managed services to stay portable across AWS/GCP/Merit
- No direct DB access across service boundaries

| `dev-merit/` | dev-merit (on-prem) | Auto-deployed by CI |
| `prod-aws/` | prod-aws (AWS eu-west-1) | Manual promotion |

## Secrets

Secrets in `dev-merit/secrets/` and `prod-aws/secrets/` are Strongbox-encrypted.
Decrypt with keys from the team's 1Password vault → `~/.strongbox_keyring`.

## Adding a New Service

1. Create `bases/<service-name>/` with deployment, service, kustomization.yaml
2. Create `dev-merit/<service-name>/` overlay (image tag, env patches)
3. Create `prod-aws/<service-name>/` overlay
4. Ensure the service is registered in the ArgoCD app config (`dev-merit/argocd-app.yaml` / `prod-aws/argocd-app.yaml`)
5. Register the service in the [service tiers list](https://wiki.uw.systems/posts/service-tiers-cqth110e)

toolUseResult.content:
# js-mono

Nx-powered TypeScript/JavaScript monorepo for UW frontend and Node services.
Node 20, Yarn (corepack), Nx for task orchestration.

## Layout

```
apps/       Deployable applications
libs/       Shared libraries (importable across apps and libs)
contracts/  Cross-team API contracts
tools/      Custom Nx generators and workspace tooling
scripts/    Setup and migration scripts
```

### Apps

| App | Description |
|---|---|
| `my-account-web` | Main customer-facing web app (Next.js) |
| `my-account-web-e2e` | Playwright end-to-end tests for my-account-web |
| `smart-tips-graphql` | GraphQL server for smart tips feature |
| `game-of-thrones-api` | (Example/test app) |

### Libs

| Lib | Description |
|---|---|
| `apollo` | Shared Apollo GraphQL client config |
| `tracing` | OpenTelemetry tracing setup |
| `fastify-uw-session-plugin` | Fastify plugin for UW session handling |
| `eslint-plugin` | Custom ESLint rules |
| `prettier-config` | Shared Prettier config |

## Setup

```bash
# Install deps
yarn install

# Decrypt Strongbox secrets (keys from JS Mono vault in 1Password → ~/.strongbox_keyring)
make decrypt-secrets

# Set npm registry token (in ~/.zshrc)
export NPM_TOKEN=<token from 1Password>
```

## Running Tasks with Nx

```bash
# Single task
yarn nx <target> <project>
yarn nx dev my-account-web
yarn nx test my-account-web

# Multiple projects
yarn nx run-many -t lint typecheck test

# Only affected by current branch
yarn nx affected -t test
```

### my-account-web Cheat Sheet

| Task | Command |
|---|---|
| Dev server | `yarn nx dev my-account-web` |
| Lint | `yarn nx lint my-account-web` |
| Type check | `yarn nx typecheck my-account-web` |
| Unit tests | `yarn nx test my-account-web` |
| E2E (Playwright) | `yarn nx e2e my-account-web-e2e` |
| GraphQL codegen | `yarn nx gql my-account-web` |
| Docker build | `yarn nx docker:build my-account-web` |
| Docker run | `yarn nx docker:run my-account-web` |

## Generating New Code

```bash
# New TypeScript library
yarn nx g @js-mono/js:library <name>
yarn nx g @js-mono/js:library <name> --publishable   # for npm release

# New Next.js app
yarn nx g @js-mono/js:next <name>
yarn nx g @js-mono/js:next <name> --appRouter        # App Router
yarn nx g @js-mono/js:next <name> --e2e              # with Playwright

# New GraphQL server
yarn nx g @js-mono/js:gqlserver <name>
```

## Migrating an Existing Package In

```bash
make move-to-js-mono
```
Interactive script — copies code into `libs/`, optionally preserves git history.

## Secrets

Env files are Strongbox-encrypted. After adding keys to `~/.strongbox_keyring`:
```bash
make decrypt-secrets
```

toolUseResult.content:
# Architecture Conventions

Engineering principles at UW. Not rigid rules but the de-facto standards the Mobile team follows.

## Core Principles

- **Microservices** — each service has a single, well-defined responsibility
- **Event-driven / event-sourced** — prefer async event passing over synchronous state sharing
- **Domain-driven design** — system boundaries reflect business structure
- **No shared state** — services must not share databases or filesystems; communicate via events

## Events

### Style
- **Informative, not imperative**: `OrderSubmitted` not `CreateOrder`
- **Fine-grained**: one event = one business-meaningful change
- **Business-meaningful**: model real-world changes, not internal implementation details

### Format
- **Protobuf** preferred for all internal events (compact, typed, code-gen friendly)
- Events are wrapped in envelopes; use `Any` for payload (more flexible than `OneOf`)
- Shared cross-team envelope format: `github.com/utilitywarehouse/event-envelope-proto`
- **JSON** used only for 3rd-party integrations or where proto is impractical
- Enum `0` value = `none` / `unknown` / `default` (never a real value)
- Enum values are prefixed with the type name (e.g. `STATUS_ACTIVE` not `ACTIVE`)

### Proto conventions (telecom-mobile)
- File: `protos/<service-name>.proto`
- Package: `<service_name>` (underscores)
- go_package: `github.com/utilitywarehouse/telecom-mobile/pkg/<service_name>`
- Generate with `make buf-gen`; don't commit generated code separately

## Transport

- **Kafka** — primary event bus for async inter-service communication
- **gRPC** — synchronous service-to-service calls (generated from protobuf service definitions)
- **GraphQL** — external and internal API surfaces (`public-graphql`, `internal-graphql`)
- **REST** — used for 3rd-party integrations; REST clients auto-generated via `make clients`

## Projectors / Sinks Pattern

Common pattern in the codebase:
- `<service>-projector` — consumes events and builds a read model / projection
- `<service>-sink` — consumes events and writes to an external system (e.g. billing, CRM)
- `<service>-connector` — bridges two systems (e.g. `bill-connector`, `mso-connector`)

## Deployment

- All services run as Docker containers in Kubernetes
- Prefer k8s over cloud-managed services to stay portable across AWS/GCP/Merit
- No direct DB access across service boundaries
